title: Datacenter Architecture
description: Practice identifying datacenter network architectures including three-tier design, spine-leaf topology, top-of-rack switching, traffic flow patterns, and datacenter infrastructure considerations.
starterCode:
# DATACENTER ARCHITECTURE EXERCISE

## Part 1: Three-Tier Architecture

1. The traditional three-tier datacenter architecture consists of core, _______, and access layers.
2. The _______ layer is the backbone of the network, providing high-speed connectivity between aggregation switches.
3. The aggregation layer is also known as the _______ layer.
4. The _______ layer connects servers and end devices to the network through rack switches.
5. In three-tier architecture, traffic between servers in different racks must traverse the _______ path up through aggregation and core.

## Part 2: Spine-Leaf Architecture

6. The spine-leaf architecture uses two layers: _______ switches and leaf switches.
7. In spine-leaf, every _______ switch connects to every spine switch, creating a full mesh.
8. Spine-leaf architecture provides _______ latency because any server is the same number of hops from any other.
9. Spine-leaf is designed to optimize _______ -west traffic between servers within the datacenter.
10. To add capacity in spine-leaf, you simply add more _______ switches to the fabric.

## Part 3: Top-of-Rack (ToR) Switching

11. In a _______ -of-Rack design, a switch is placed at the top of each server rack.
12. ToR switching reduces _______ congestion by connecting servers within the rack locally.
13. Each ToR switch connects to the next layer via _______ links to aggregation or spine switches.
14. An alternative to ToR is _______ -of-Row (EoR) switching, where switches serve an entire row of racks.
15. ToR switches typically provide _______ Ethernet ports (1G/10G/25G) to the servers below them.

## Part 4: Traffic Flow Patterns

16. _______ -south traffic flows between the datacenter and external networks (clients, internet).
17. East-_______ traffic flows between servers within the datacenter.
18. Modern applications generate more east-west traffic due to _______ architectures and distributed computing.
19. _______ -leaf architecture is optimized for east-west traffic patterns common in modern datacenters.
20. North-south traffic typically passes through _______ and security appliances at the network edge.

## Part 5: Datacenter Infrastructure

21. _______ distribution units (PDUs) distribute electrical power to equipment in server racks.
22. Hot aisle/cold aisle is a _______ management strategy to efficiently cool datacenter equipment.
23. A datacenter _______ rating (1-4) from the Uptime Institute classifies facility redundancy and availability.
24. _______ redundancy means there are duplicate components so a single failure causes no downtime.
25. Datacenter _______ refers to the physical arrangement of racks, rows, and cable pathways.

## Part 6: Scenario - Datacenter Tier Classification

Your CTO asks you to evaluate whether the company datacenter meets Tier III standards.

26. The Uptime Institute defines _______ tiers (I through IV) of datacenter reliability and redundancy.
27. A Tier III "Concurrently _______" datacenter allows maintenance without downtime, requiring N+1 redundancy.
28. Tier IV datacenters are "_______ Tolerant," surviving any single infrastructure failure without impact on IT load.
29. Hot aisle/cold aisle _______ is a physical design strategy that separates hot exhaust air from cool intake air.
30. The datacenter _______ Usage Effectiveness (PUE) metric measures total facility power divided by IT equipment power.

## Part 7: Scenario - Spine-Leaf Network Design

A cloud service provider is designing a new datacenter network using spine-leaf architecture.

31. In spine-leaf topology, every _______ switch connects to every spine switch, providing consistent latency.
32. The maximum number of hops between any two servers in a spine-leaf design is always _______ (spine→leaf→spine).
33. Spine-leaf eliminates the traditional _______ layer, replacing the 3-tier model (core/distribution/access) with 2 tiers.
34. _______ links between spine and leaf switches use ECMP (Equal-Cost Multi-Path) for load balancing across all paths.
35. In a VXLAN-based spine-leaf fabric, the spine switches serve as _______ route reflectors for distributing MAC/IP reachability information.
solution:
# SOLUTION

## Part 1: Three-Tier Architecture

1. The traditional three-tier datacenter architecture consists of core, **aggregation**, and access layers.
2. The **core** layer is the backbone of the network, providing high-speed connectivity between aggregation switches.
3. The aggregation layer is also known as the **distribution** layer.
4. The **access** layer connects servers and end devices to the network through rack switches.
5. In three-tier architecture, traffic between servers in different racks must traverse the **full** path up through aggregation and core.

## Part 2: Spine-Leaf Architecture

6. The spine-leaf architecture uses two layers: **spine** switches and leaf switches.
7. In spine-leaf, every **leaf** switch connects to every spine switch, creating a full mesh.
8. Spine-leaf architecture provides **predictable** latency because any server is the same number of hops from any other.
9. Spine-leaf is designed to optimize **east** -west traffic between servers within the datacenter.
10. To add capacity in spine-leaf, you simply add more **spine** switches to the fabric.

## Part 3: Top-of-Rack (ToR) Switching

11. In a **Top** -of-Rack design, a switch is placed at the top of each server rack.
12. ToR switching reduces **cabling** congestion by connecting servers within the rack locally.
13. Each ToR switch connects to the next layer via **uplink** links to aggregation or spine switches.
14. An alternative to ToR is **End** -of-Row (EoR) switching, where switches serve an entire row of racks.
15. ToR switches typically provide **copper** Ethernet ports (1G/10G/25G) to the servers below them.

## Part 4: Traffic Flow Patterns

16. **North** -south traffic flows between the datacenter and external networks (clients, internet).
17. East-**west** traffic flows between servers within the datacenter.
18. Modern applications generate more east-west traffic due to **microservices** architectures and distributed computing.
19. **Spine** -leaf architecture is optimized for east-west traffic patterns common in modern datacenters.
20. North-south traffic typically passes through **firewalls** and security appliances at the network edge.

## Part 5: Datacenter Infrastructure

21. **Power** distribution units (PDUs) distribute electrical power to equipment in server racks.
22. Hot aisle/cold aisle is a **thermal** management strategy to efficiently cool datacenter equipment.
23. A datacenter **tier** rating (1-4) from the Uptime Institute classifies facility redundancy and availability.
24. **N+1** redundancy means there are duplicate components so a single failure causes no downtime.
25. Datacenter **layout** refers to the physical arrangement of racks, rows, and cable pathways.

## Part 6: Scenario - Datacenter Tier Classification

Your CTO asks you to evaluate whether the company datacenter meets Tier III standards.

26. The Uptime Institute defines **four** tiers (I through IV) of datacenter reliability and redundancy.
27. A Tier III "Concurrently **Maintainable**" datacenter allows maintenance without downtime, requiring N+1 redundancy.
28. Tier IV datacenters are "**Fault** Tolerant," surviving any single infrastructure failure without impact on IT load.
29. Hot aisle/cold aisle **containment** is a physical design strategy that separates hot exhaust air from cool intake air.
30. The datacenter **Power** Usage Effectiveness (PUE) metric measures total facility power divided by IT equipment power.

## Part 7: Scenario - Spine-Leaf Network Design

A cloud service provider is designing a new datacenter network using spine-leaf architecture.

31. In spine-leaf topology, every **leaf** switch connects to every spine switch, providing consistent latency.
32. The maximum number of hops between any two servers in a spine-leaf design is always **three** (spine→leaf→spine).
33. Spine-leaf eliminates the traditional **distribution** layer, replacing the 3-tier model (core/distribution/access) with 2 tiers.
34. **All** links between spine and leaf switches use ECMP (Equal-Cost Multi-Path) for load balancing across all paths.
35. In a VXLAN-based spine-leaf fabric, the spine switches serve as **BGP** route reflectors for distributing MAC/IP reachability information.
hints: Three-tier architecture: Core (backbone) → Aggregation/Distribution (policies) → Access (server connections).
Spine-leaf creates a full mesh between spine and leaf layers, ensuring consistent hop count and latency.
East-west traffic is server-to-server within the datacenter; north-south is datacenter-to-external.
Top-of-Rack (ToR) places a switch in each rack, reducing long cable runs to a central switch.
End-of-Row (EoR) uses fewer switches that serve multiple racks in a row, but requires longer cables.
Spine-leaf is the modern standard for datacenters with heavy east-west traffic (microservices, VMs).
PDUs (Power Distribution Units) distribute and sometimes monitor electrical power to rack equipment.
Hot aisle/cold aisle arrangements direct airflow efficiently through equipment for optimal cooling.
Uptime Institute Tier ratings range from 1 (basic) to 4 (fault tolerant, 99.995% uptime).
Uplink ports on ToR switches connect to the aggregation/spine layer using higher-speed fiber links.

testCases.description: Identifies Fault Tolerant for Tier IV
